coredata.dc:title: Enhanced optical properties and dark I-V characteristics of silicon nanowire-carbon quantum dots heterostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Mishra, Smruti Medha
Dey, Suman
Singha, Tukai
Mandal, Subhankar
Dehury, Asish K.
Chaudhary, Yatendra S.
Satpati, Biswarup
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a silicon nanowire (SiNW) array-carbon quantum dot (CQD) heterostructure photovoltaic device via direct coating of CQD on chemically-etched SiNW arrays aided by Ag. By using carbon quantum dots layer as a competent element for surface passivation and modification for SiNWs, the solar cells efficiency is improved. The 1.6 times absorption enhancement has been recorded for nitrogen doped CQD decorated pyramidal SiNW heterostructure in comparison to that of CQDs coated silicon nanowires on the planar surfaces. Inclusion of nitrogen doped CQDs into the pyramidal SiNW arrays gives enhanced absorption intensity which can act as a good absorber layer in solar cell. The heterostructure also displays a significant photoluminescence in the blue region as probed by using time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) technique which provide the insight into the recombination mechanism in the synthesized heterostructures and is discussed in detail.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanowires
Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Band gap tuning
Photoluminescence
Carrier lifetime

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of SiNWs on planar Si
Fabrication of SiNWs on textured silicon (pyramidal Si) surface
Formation of SiNWs on pyramidal Si
Morphological observations of SiNW-CQDs hybrids (FESEM study)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India
Institute of Minerals and Materials Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors sincerely thank Dr. Bikash Kumar Jena, CSIR-IMMT, Bhubaneswar for his help in extending the UV–Vis measurements facility, Prof. Supratic Chakraborty, SINP, Kolkata for availing the I-V measurements, Prof. Satyajit Hazra, SINP, Kolkata for availing the XPS facility. The authors also thank Mr. Debraj Dey for his technical support in the SEM operation and Mr. Goutam Sarkar for his technical support in the XPS measurements. S.M.M. acknowledges Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing research fellowship.

The authors sincerely thank Dr. Bikash Kumar Jena, CSIR-IMMT, Bhubaneswar for his help in extending the UV–Vis measurements facility, Prof. Supratic Chakraborty, SINP, Kolkata for availing the I-V measurements, Prof. Satyajit Hazra, SINP, Kolkata for availing the XPS facility. The authors also thank Mr. Debraj Dey for his technical support in the SEM operation and Mr. Goutam Sarkar for his technical support in the XPS measurements. S.M.M. acknowledges Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the fabrication strategy of the SiNW-CQD heterostructures.
TEM image of the as synthesized CQDs and NCQDs respectively; inset shows the HRTEM image of individual QDs with respective size distribution.
C 1s core-level spectra of (a) CQDs, (b) NCQDs, and (c) N 1s core-level spectra of NCQDs.
SEM images of (top left) SINW arrays; (top right) CQDs layer; (bottom left) SiNWs-CQDs heterostructure; (bottom right) Magnified view of SiNWs-CQDs hetero-structure.

Cross-sectional TEM image of (a) SiNWs, (b) SiNWs-CQDs Hybrid, (c) TEM image of a single hybrid, where inset shows the IFFT image of (c) and (d) shows the HRTEM image of the marked portion in (c). (e), (f) and (g) are EDX elemental maps of Si-K, C-K, and their composite respectively.

(a) Cross Sectional TEM image of SiNWs-NCQDs Hybrid, (b) STEM-HAADF image, (c), (d), (e) and (f) are EDX elemental maps of Si-K, N-K, C-K and their composite respectively, and (g) line profiles of the individual elements along the line marked in (b).
(a) Current density plotted for all the device structures under dark in full range (log scale), (b) Forward current represented for all the samples.
Recorded absorbance spectra for all the synthesized samples.

(a) Tauc plot shows the variation in optical band gap value for the SiNWs, P-SiNWs and their QDs decorated heterostructure, and (b) Variations of band gap with the decorations of CQDs and NCQDs onto the SiNWs and P-SiNWs.
Steady state photoluminescence spectra displaying the variance in luminescence distribution.
(a) Time resolved photoluminescence plot for bare and QDs decorated silicon nanowires, and (b) Plot of carrier lifetime calculated against each synthesized sample.
Extracted series resistance, shunt resistance and ideality factor from the dark I-V curve.

head.ce:title: Enhanced optical properties and dark I-V characteristics of silicon nanowire-carbon quantum dots heterostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Surface Physics & Material Science Division, Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics, A CI of Homi Bhabha National Institute, 1/AF Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700064, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Surface Physics & Material Science Division, Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics
A CI of Homi Bhabha National Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSurface Physics & Material Science Division, Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics, A CI of Homi Bhabha National Institute, 1/AF Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700064, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Chemistry Department, CSIR-Institute of Minerals and Materials Technology, Bhubaneswar 751013, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Institute of Minerals and Materials Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bMaterials Chemistry Department, CSIR-Institute of Minerals and Materials Technology, Bhubaneswar 751013, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a silicon nanowire (SiNW) array-carbon quantum dot (CQD) heterostructure photovoltaic device via direct coating of CQD on chemically-etched SiNW arrays aided by Ag. By using carbon quantum dots layer as a competent element for surface passivation and modification for SiNWs, the solar cells efficiency is improved. The 1.6 times absorption enhancement has been recorded for nitrogen doped CQD decorated pyramidal SiNW heterostructure in comparison to that of CQDs coated silicon nanowires on the planar surfaces. Inclusion of nitrogen doped CQDs into the pyramidal SiNW arrays gives enhanced absorption intensity which can act as a good absorber layer in solar cell. The heterostructure also displays a significant photoluminescence in the blue region as probed by using time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) technique which provide the insight into the recombination mechanism in the synthesized heterostructures and is discussed in detail.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar cells are in ever-increasing demand as a means of harvesting clean, renewable solar radiation energy. Nowadays, crystalline silicon (Si) continues to dominate the bulk of the economic solar cells due to its abundant material resources, benign nature, high efficiency as well as long-term stability [1]. Recently the commitment of room-temperature fabrication and solution processing capability has fascinated the interest of many researchers in hybrid solar cells. Silicon nanowires (SiNWs) in ensembles have attracted much attention and have been studied for a long time to serve as the upcoming building blocks in solar cells, sensors, transistors, and photo-detectors [2,3]. Indeed the silicon-based strategies are preferred because of material richness and nontoxicity at a high level. The high surface-to-volume ratio and exclusive quasi one-dimensional structure is the primary reason due to which silicon nanowire-based devices have gained a lot of attention nowadays [4]. To fabricate silicon nanowires, several methods have been devised, including the laser ablation approach, the vapor-liquid-solid method, and the oxide-assisted growth technique [5]. The main drawbacks of these approaches are that they often need the use of ignitable Si source precursors, high thermal processing, and also high vacuum environmental condition. To overcome these challenges, an alternative and productive route has been suggested which is metal-assisted chemical etching (MACE). It is indeed a simple, cost-effective, and environment friendly technique, which is based on the electrochemical redox reactions as well as selective chemical etching methods that enable to get vertical SiNWs on the Si substrate with several tens of micro-meters length [6]. The inclusion of quantum dots (QDs) into solar cells has been suggested as a promising approach to boost device conversion efficiency [7]. The inclusion of QDs to the solar cell's active region permits one to engineer the effective band gap of the material and elongate the absorption spectrum. It could be utilized to create an isolated intermediate band within the band gap of the host material to captivate photons with energy below the host material energy gap or to increase the photocurrent of a homogeneous solar cell [8]. The vertically aligned silicon nanowires can enhance the light absorption dramatically due to exceptional light trapping ability of QDs [9,10]. Besides these the NW-QD hetero-structure can be fabricated on the silicon substrates, which makes it favorable for low-cost and high-efficiency solar cells. Prior to practical applications, one can evaluate the device parameters and analyze the performance of the solar cell by studying the electrical properties of solar cells in dark condition. The I-V characteristics in the dark condition is an important tool to assess their performance and are commonly used to study the electrical properties of the photovoltaic cells, providing an effective means of determining the key performance parameters without requiring a solar simulator [11]. Also, the dark characteristics are one of the simple ways to test the quality of a junction [12]. Several characteristics of SiNWs make them an efficient material for photovoltaic applications [13,14]. The anti-reflecting property is the most important one. SiNWs have really low reflectance, which means they absorb maximum of the incident light. The second valuable characteristic is the high aspect-ratio. This allows them to captivate enough sunlight with thinner silicon structures, thus lowering the cost. The third feature of SiNWs is the self-cleaning property. SiNWs are hydrophobic in nature due to their distinctive structures, revealing a property referred to as the self-cleaning effect [15]. In environment, dust particles get together on the surface of the photovoltaic units after a couple of days of use. As a result, the sunlight is blocked which lowers the efficiency of a solar cell. The nanowire solar cells overcome this difficulty using the self-cleaning effect. One more important requirement for photovoltaic applications is that all of the sunlight that is absorbed be converted into free charge carriers [16]. And the incorporation of QDs onto the SiNWs make them best suited for the photovoltaic application as it enhances the performance of the cells [17]. Even though the fabrication and optical properties of planar SiNW-CQDs hybrid nanostructure have been reported earlier, the performance of pyramidal SiNW-nitrogen doped carbon QDs heterostructures has not been investigated yet.

In this work, we have fabricated two kinds of hybrid structures namely on planar Si (SiNWs-CQDs) and pyramidal Si (P-SiNWs-CQDs), and in both the cases we have coated with undoped CQDs and nitrogen doped CQDs (NCQDs) for Si NWs modifications (Fig. 1 ). We have confirmed the presence of QDs and its doping by XPS analysis. After making the hybrid structures, we put forward those nanostructures to device fabrication and performed their dark I-V characteristics measurement. And we have also done the UV–Visible spectroscopy to analyze the absorption enhancement for each nanostructure and hybrid. In order to underpin the charge carrier dynamics, the detailed measurements using the photoluminescence spectrophotometer have been undertaken and have discussed in detail.

Silver Nitrate (AgNO3), Glucose (C6H12O6), Citric Acid (C6H8O7) were purchased from Merck. Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), Hydrofluoric acid (HF), Hydrogen Peroxide (H2O2), ethanol, Ammonia solution (NH3. H2O) and Isopropyl Alcohol were all A.R grade in purity. The water used throughout this work was deionized water (DI water).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of SiNWs on planar Si
ce:section.ce:para: This method involves two main steps which are electro-less (silver) deposition on the Si wafer followed by chemical etching. In a typical metal-assisted chemical etching process, a Si substrate sheltered by a noble metal is subjected to an etchant composed of HF and an oxidative agent. Typically, the Si underneath the noble metal is etched much faster than the Si without noble metal coverage. As a result, the noble metal sinks into the Si substrate, generating Si nanowires.

Si (100) [p-type] planar and pyramidal substrates were used to deposit Ag nanoparticles by electro-less deposition. Prior to etching, the Si substrates were cleaned by ultra-sonication in acetone, ethanol, and DI water respectively for 5 min. Then these substrates were immersed in the dilute HF solution for about 30 s to remove the oxidant. First step was to deposit a layer of silver on the substrate and that was performed by immersing the substrates in the solution composed of 0.02 M AgNO3 and 4.8 M HF for 1 min and this was carried out at room temperature.

After the successful Ag deposition, the next step is to etch the Ag deposited wafers. The etchant solution composed of 4.8 M HF and 0.2 M H2O2. And the etching continued for about 5 min. Finally, here we found straight planar Silicon Nanowires formed on the substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of SiNWs on textured silicon (pyramidal Si) surface

ce:section.ce:para: To produce SiNW arrays on textured Si (pyramidal), a piece of Si (100) substrates were first ultrasonically cleaned for 5 min using ethanol, acetone, and deionized water. Following cleaning, the substrates were etched using the saw damage removal (SDR) technique reported previously. In this method substrates were dipped in 30 wt% NaOH solution and maintained at 75 °C for 3 min removing layers 5 to 6 µm thick from both surfaces of the silicon substrate. To produce SiNW arrays on the textured surface, the substrates were then submerged in a mixed solution of NaOH (2 wt%) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 10%) in deionized water for 45 min at 80 °C. As these structures resemble pyramids they are called as pyramidal Si (P-Si)) arrays. The sizes of the Si pyramids grow with the increase in immersion time. After rinsing with ethanol and deionized water, the substrates were put into vacuum desiccators until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of SiNWs on pyramidal Si
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure follows the same step as discussed for the formation of SiNWs on planar Si which includes the deposition of Ag NPs and thereby the growth of nanowires.

Carbon quantum dots were prepared by dissolving glucose in deionized water (50 mL) to form a solution of 1 mole/L. The solution of sodium hydroxide, NaOH (50 mL, 1 mole/L) was added to the solution of glucose and then the mixed solution was treated ultrasonically for about 4 long hours. Then the raw solution was oven dried at 80 °C for 6 h. The dried sample was freely dispersed in water without any further modifications.

2 g of pure citric acid was added into the solution of 60 mL of ammonia solution (NH3. H2O). The mixed solution was placed onto the heating platform for about 30 min at 300 °C. 50 mL of NaOH solution was added to it once it gets cooled down to room temperature. Then the solution was magnetically stirred for about 10 min and then ultrasonically shocked for 10 min. Then, finally the solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min.

The hybrid nanostructures were fabricated by the incorporation of quantum dots (QDs) onto the silicon nanowire surface. The deposition of QDs made possible by spin-coating method, where the QD solution was spin coated at a speed of 1000 rpm for 3 min. Then the nanowires substrates containing CQDs were heated at 300 °C.

The device structure has been fabricated by making top and bottom metal contacts by electron-beam (e-beam) evaporation technique by depositing platinum (Pt) metal on both sides (about 50 nm thick). By this way, we fabricated four devices per sample to perform the electrical measurements.

Field emission electron microscope (FESEM) was used for initial characterization in order to analyze the overall morphology and transmission electron microscope (TEM) was carried out to do the extensive structural analysis. For XTEM measurement two cut sections (3 mm × 6 mm) were joined face to face and placed within a brass tube from which a number of discs of 0.5 mm thickness were sliced. The cut discs were subsequently mechanically thinned, double-dimpled, and finally milled by Ar+ ions utilizing a precision ion polishing system (PIPS, GATAN Inc.) at an energy of 3.5 keV while being cleaned at 1.5 keV. The TEM (FEI Tecnai G2 F30-ST) was performed at 300 keV which is additionally equipped with high angle annular dark field (HAADF) detector from Fischione (Model 3000) and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) attachment for compositional analysis [18,19]. The formation of QD structures were confirmed from TEM observations by dropping the solution onto the Cu grid. The XPS measurements were also performed for the chemical analysis of the QDs and understanding the surface treatment of the QDs. For the XPS measurements, monochromatic Al Kα X-ray of 1486.6 eV photon energy was used as a source and the corresponding spectrometer energy resolution was ∼0.8 eV in an MXPS set up (Omicron Nanotechnology; base pressure ∼ 1.0 × 10−9) [20,21]. UV- Visible spectroscopy has been carried out for analyzing their respective absorption profile using UV- Vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, UV-2600). Dark IV measurements were used to examine the electrical properties of photovoltaic (PV) cells, offering a way to ascertain the fundamental performance parameters without requiring a solar simulator. For electrical measurements a metallic film has been evaporated on the as-deposited film. In this work, platinum was evaporated on the films for electrical contact by an electron-beam (e-beam) evaporation system with e-beam source manufactured by Telemark (Model name: 211). Keithley 4200-SCS semiconductor parameter analyzer (SPA) is used to measure I-V characteristics [22]. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a Fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS 980) at room temperature [23,24].

The TEM images of CQDs and NCQDs are shown in Fig. 2 , which makes it abundantly evident that the as synthesized QDs are quasi-spheres with very well dispersion. Compared to CQDs, which have a wider size distribution and an average size of 6 nm, NCQDs have a narrower size distribution with an average size of 4 nm. The insets are the HRTEM images indicating the crystallinity with lattice spacing 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) plane of carbon particle [25].

XPS analysis of the as synthesized CQDs and NCQDs is shown in Fig. 3 . The survey scan of CQDs shows that CQDs are mainly comprised of carbon (C 1s), and oxygen (O 1s). No indication of any other residual element, highlighting the QDs' purity. The survey scan of NCQDs shows that NCQDs are composed of carbon (C 1s), oxygen (O 1s), and nitrogen (N 1s) respectively. Fig. 3 depicts the deconvolution of the C 1s peak, with the major peak corresponding to CC bonded carbon at 284.8 eV, whereas the peaks at 285.6 eV, 287.3 eV and 289.1 eV are ascribed to CO, C=O and OC=O, respectively [26,27]. One of every four carbon atoms was functionalized with oxygen that reveals the CQDs have a high degree of functionalization.

Deconvolution of C 1s peak of NCQDs is shown in Fig. 3b, where the main peak at 284.6 eV corresponds to CC bonded carbon and the peak at 285.8 eV corresponds to the carbon atoms bonded to nitrogen atoms, whereas peaks at 286.5, 286.7 and 288.2 eV are attributed to CO, C=O and C=ON respectively [28,29]. Deconvolution of N 1 s peak of NCQDs is shown in Fig. 3c, where peaks at 397.9 eV, 399.2 eV and 400.3 eV correspond to pyridinic Ns, 1°/2° amino Ns and pyrolic Ns, respectively. Presence of N 1s confirms the incorporation of nitrogen atoms to the carbon quantum dots. In CQDs, it contains a carbon core, and the functional groups that contain oxygen are on the surface of the carbon core and in case of NCQDs, N atoms have been added into the carbon nuclear lattice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological observations of SiNW-CQDs hybrids (FESEM study)
ce:section.ce:para: The overall morphology of all the fabricated nanostructures and heterostructures are shown in Fig. 4 .

The nanowires are vertically aligned to the substrate and the filling ratios are very high such that they form closely packed nanowires. Such vertical structure exceptionally facilitates superior light absorption and photon management [30]. From the magnified view of the heterostructure, it can clearly be seen that the QDs layer are well incorporated on to the formed silicon nanowires.

The bright-field TEM image of CQD layered SiNWs shows that the SiNWs are fully encapsulated by a continuous CQD film, Fig. 5 . EDX spectra recorded confirm the presence of carbon, and silicon, and the elemental mapping as shown in Fig. 5(e–g), further reveals how the elements are distributed along the hetero-structure. From the composite map presented in Fig. 5g, it is clearly seen that high density of CQDs form a continuous layer on and around the silicon nanowires. Fig. 5d is the HRTEM image of the marked part in Fig. 5c, where carbon quantum dots are visibly seen around the silicon nanowires.

Fig. 6 shows TEM image of nitrogen doped silicon nanowires which displays similar kind of deposition of quantum dots on nanowires as in the case of SiNWs-CQDs. The presence of Si, C, and N have been confirmed from the elemental maps taken and the line profile (bottom right) depicts the distribution of elements along the line marked in the Fig. 6b (top right).

For all other samples, those were deposited on pyramidal silicon substrates like, P-SiNWs-CQDs and P-SiNWs-NCQDs, we found similar kind of deposition of quantum dots layer on the silicon nanowires. Due to pyramidal structure of the silicon wafer surface itself, the nanowires formed on this substrate are different in heights (Fig. S1 in the supporting information).

The electrical parameters of solar cells and modules can be determined with great accuracy using dark I-V characterization [31], and it has a wealth of advantages over light I–V characterization, including: it is not impacted by changes in the light intensity, doesn't necessitate a quick measurement method; and is not impacted by shade or other optical elements that typically lead to distortions [32].

An outstanding rectifying behavior can be easily seen from the usual I-V curve of the SiNW-QDs hybrid combinations shown in Fig. 7 . Moreover, we notice a drop in the reverse current and a significant rise in the forward current when compared to the QDs-free SiNW sample, indicating that the hetero-junction potential barrier at the interface has improved the rectifying behavior after QDs modification. The dark saturation current density (Js) is an extremely vital parameter which distinguishes one from another. Js is a degree of recombination in a device. As a result, a diode with greater recombination will have greater Js.
Reduced recombination can result in low saturation current density and high open circuit voltage [8].

In comparison to the device having nanowires without QDs coating, the QDs coated heterostructure shows low saturation current density which results from reduction in recombination. Based on the dark I-V (ln I-V) curve, the diode ideality factor (n) has been estimated and enlisted in the Table 1 along with series resistance (Rs) and shunt resistance (Rsh). Interpolating the characteristics near zero voltage where the I-V characteristics will be a clear straight line. Its inverse slope will be the shunt resistance, Rsh. Rs was also calculated likewise at higher current regions. Lower shunt resistance is seen when the CQD layer is lacking from the device, which in turn gives lower fill factor. Ideally, Rsh is as high as possible in order to avoid the current leakage and Rs is minimized to prevent the efficiency losses caused by increased charge carrier recombination. Rs originate from the energetic barrier at the interfaces as well as bulk resistance in the layers, whereas Rsh takes into account the presence of alternate current channels throughout the photovoltaic cell. The lower series resistance dissipates less power from the harvested electrical solar power from the incident solar radiation on the solar cell. It is so that the dissipated power in series resistance = I x Rs 2 where I denotes the solar cell's current. Therefore, the power conversation efficiency (PCE) will be higher. The same is valid for the shunt resistance Rsh. The power dissipated in Rsh= Vj 2/ Rsh. For the same junction voltage Vj, the dissipated power decreases with increasing Rsh [33,34]. Therefore, the PCE will be higher. So, in this case we can say lower Rs and higher Rsh gives decrease in the dissipated power. Thus, power conversion efficiency will be higher [34]. Device named as P-SiNW-NCQD has low series resistance and high shunt resistance, which in turn results into higher PCE with respect to all other devices. This is mostly attributed to the NCQDs' strong passivation effect, which significantly lowers the surface recombination.

Fig. 8 shows the absorption spectra of as prepared hybrid nanostructures such as SiNW, P-SiNW, SiNW-CQD, P-SiNW-CQD, SiNW-NCQD, and P-SiNW-NCQD. The quantum dots coated hybrid nanostructures display well improved light absorption with a tail extending to 800 nm and providing a full coverage of visible region in comparison to the bare silicon nanowires. And also, hybrid nanostructure fabricated upon textured silicon wafer i.e., pyramidal Si shows enhanced absorption than that of nanostructures upon non-textured Si wafer [35].

And as we know, CQDs show exceptional optical absorption. But when CQDs doped with nitrogen thereby forming nitrogen doped CQDs, NCQDs gives much better absorption. Existence of amino groups present in NCQDs act as auxochrome. And CQDs in combination with some auxochromes change their absorption intensity and results in enhancement in absorption [36]. The absorption enhancement has been recorded for P-SiNW-NCQD by a factor of 1.6 in comparison to that of the CQDs coated silicon nanowires on the planar surfaces. Inclusion of nitrogen doped CQDs into the pyramidal SiNW arrays gives enhanced absorption intensity which can act as a good absorber layer in solar cell modules. The optical band gap of a semiconductor is the minimum energy necessary to shift an electron from its valence band to the conduction band which can then contribute to conduction. For each synthesized nanostructure, the Tauc plot was further extracted for the band gap determination (Fig. 9 ).

The Tauc relation for direct band gap materials is given by the relation:
α h ν =
(
E g
− − h ν
)
n
where α denotes the linear absorption coefficient, A denotes the proportionality constant, and n is equivalent to ½ for direct band gap materials [37].

The band gap determined for P-SiNW-NCQD to be the lowest of all synthesized samples which is 3 eV. The decrease in optical band gap can be attributed to the development of compensatory energy states caused by nitrogen dopant elements, which further shifts the conduction band edges in heterostructures containing N-doped CQDs [38]. In bulk, the fusion of a cluster of adjacent energy levels of several atoms and molecules results in the formation of bands. As particle size reaches the nanoscale, where each particle is composed of just a couple of atoms or molecules, the number of energy levels that overlap reduces and the band's width shrinks. This causes an increase in band gap among the valence band and the conduction band.

Steady state photoluminescence spectra for all the synthesized samples were recorded at 310 nm excitation wavelength as shown in the Fig. 10 . The radiative recombination of the photo generated electrons and holes is the origin of the photoluminescence, which can occur from band to band as well as from the defect states to band [39]. As can be seen in Fig. 10 from the PL spectroscopic observations, there are two dominating peaks. However, by deconvoluting the spectra by Gaussian fitting, four peaks are identifiable at 465, 515, 585, and 630 nm as shown in the above Fig. 10. The blue emission doesn't originate from deep level defects but from band-to-band transition with a quantum confinement effect [40,41,42,43].

As to the origin of peak at 515 nm, it is related to the small Si crystals. The silicon host dioxide matrix, sometimes referred to as the surface SiSi dimers, is what gives the PL emission peak at 584 nm [44]. The silicon host dioxide matrix is an inherent SiNWs PL peak. And the luminescence band at around 630 nm is due to the surface states and SiOx based interface states in silicon nanowires [30–32,8, 33–41,44,45]. This appears probably due to the exciton confined at the Si-oxide interface. The enhancement in blue emission is obviously due to the Si dangling bonds being passivated during the hydrothermal treatment, which reduces the amount of non-radiative centers and further suppresses the non-radiative process [46].

The PL intensity of the P-SiNWs i.e., nanowires grown on the textured wafer is considerably higher than that of planar wafer as the pyramidal texture increases the light absorption. When compared to planar SiNWs, textured surfaces are able to couple light more efficiently into the Si and boost the absorption capacity of the devices. As a result, the amount of emitted photons will rise drastically. The enhancement in the PL may be due to (i) better absorption by pyramidal surface (absorption factor) and (ii) texturization (area factor) [35]. Therefore, enhancement can be ascribed primarily to the geometry of the pyramidal surface. Considering the contribution of quantum dots, the extrinsic surface states are introduced by heteroatoms (N, P, B, S etc.) and surface functional groups (Oxygen / Nitrogen based groups etc.) and in CQDs, which can offer emissive trap spots or density of states for photo-excited electrons hence regulate the PL process [47]. The carbon core structure in these samples is similar in size, while the surface state (explicitly the extent of oxidation) varies amongst samples. The band gap decreased as more oxygen species were incorporated into their surface structures. Hence, surface states are the dominant factor monitoring the PL variations. Oxygen, nitrogen and associated chemical bonds will create impurity levels in the band gap, which will modify the emission spectra of the samples. There could be a route for energy transfer among the carbon core and the surface state. For QDs decorated P-SiNWs, the emission intensity decreases. Such quenching after QDs modification suggests the decay of excited electrons via non-radiative pathways, implying that recombination of photo-generated excitons is suppressed after QDs modification [39]. Henceforth, it is evident that the CQDs functionalization facilitates improved charge separation thereby decreasing recombination losses and the quenching can be ascribed to the internal absorption with concentrations of CQD.

Fig. 11 shows the decay profile of the sample at 468 nm emission wavelength and 310 nm excitation wavelength. Both, the decay curve and fitted line are shown in the Fig. 11. The curve was fitted by a bi-exponential fitting which refers that the compound might have two relaxation states with values given in Table S1. The corresponding equation is
I
( t )
=
I 0
+
I 1
e x p
(
− t /
τ 1
)
+
I 2
e x p
(
− t /
τ 2
)
where I0 and I(t) are the initial intensities and at time t respectively. I1 and I2, are constants, τ1 and τ2 denotes the exponential components’ lifetimes. The average lifetime value for this molecule is calculated by using the average lifetime formula of the average lifetime.
τ
a v g
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B
1
τ 1
+
B
2
τ 2
where B1 and B2 are the respective amplitudes of the lifetime components.

The photoluminescence lifetime of SiNW is less than P-SiNW. Upon modification with quantum dots, the lifetime increases due to efficient charge transfer across the hetero-interface. It is seen that the P-SiNW-NCQDs heterostructure has a longer average decay time (2.12 ns) than that of others, inferring an efficient charge transfer across the interface which results in delayed decay, and hence longer lifetime is seen [39]. The lifetime of NCQD-incorporated molecules is more than CQD due to the presence of defect states again upon nitrogen doping during the synthesis [48].

In summary, the SiNW-QDs heterostructures by taking both, planar and pyramidal SiNWs and also CQDs and NCQDs have been successfully fabricated via a simple and economically viable approach. The investigation of morphology reveals that the nanowires are fully wrapped by the quantum dots layer in all the synthesized samples. From the electrical parameters derived from the dark I-V curve, device having pyramidal SiNW and NCQDs has been predicted to give higher power conversion efficiency (PCE). SiNW-CQDs heterostructure offers mostly two beneficial effects: (i) effective suppression of recombination, & (ii) improved optical absorption. The absorption enhancement has been recorded for P-SiNW-NCQD by a factor of 1.6 in comparison to that of the CQDs coated silicon nanowires on the planar surfaces. Inclusion of nitrogen doped CQDs into the pyramidal SiNW arrays gives enhanced absorption intensity which can act as a good absorber layer in solar cell modules. The optical band gap of the heterostructures shows tunability with the encapsulation of quantum dots layer and such band gap engineering is desirable for solar cell applications. In the case of a QDs coated heterostructure, quantum dots anchoring on the silicon nanowire surface reduces the density of trapped states, thus allowing efficient electron transfer across the hetero-interface. Defect engineering or surface modification through the use of appropriate synthesis procedures is a method of tailoring the material's photoluminescence properties. Thus, the heterostructure has shown excellent electrical and optical properties and henceforth, can be a potential candidate in the fields of photovoltaic devices and optoelectronic integrated circuits. This study paves the way for further investigations to use the method of activation of Si surface with quantum dots in the photovoltaic devices.
Morphology of the pyramidal SiNWs, absorbance spectra of CQDs and NCQDs, normalized PL spectra of the synthesized heterostructures, and calculated carrier lifetime of all the samples (Table S1).

Smruti Medha Mishra: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Suman Dey: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Tukai Singha: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Validation. Subhankar Mandal: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Asish K. Dehury: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yatendra S. Chaudhary: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Biswarup Satpati: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely thank Dr. Bikash Kumar Jena, CSIR-IMMT, Bhubaneswar for his help in extending the UV–Vis measurements facility, Prof. Supratic Chakraborty, SINP, Kolkata for availing the I-V measurements, Prof. Satyajit Hazra, SINP, Kolkata for availing the XPS facility. The authors also thank Mr. Debraj Dey for his technical support in the SEM operation and Mr. Goutam Sarkar for his technical support in the XPS measurements. S.M.M. acknowledges Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR), India for providing research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2023.112262.
